chapters.title: A potential without a ceiling
chapters.text:
Getting into the Navy SEALs was not easy. All but one recruiter turned David Goggins away. Only Petty Officer Steven Schaljo saw something in him. To even have a chance at his dream, David had to drop a hundred and six pounds and spend months preparing for the ASVAB, which he eventually passed. Still, that was only the start of a long and grueling road. Candidates for the SEALs must pass special trials to earn their place. Hell Week is one of them, and the name is no accident. While participants carry out seemingly impossible tasks in water and on land, instructors try to break them in every way they can. David had to survive three Hell Weeks to reach his goal. During the first, he came down with double pneumonia. His second try ended early because of a damaged knee. Even so, David took away a lot from the experience.

Everything in life is a mind game, as David Goggins has said.

During SEAL training, he created the Taking Souls tactic, which helps you tap into a second wind. David used it in the toughest moments to push back against the instructors' mental and physical pressure. For instance, he hummed a song or smiled through the pain. His goal was not to suppress or anger the instructors. It was to keep inner control.

In moments of despair, remember your goal and keep striving.

The Calloused Mind was his other tactic for dealing with a hard situation. It helped him push through the second Hell Week with a broken knee. Picture it. He performed the required kicks in the water, fighting through excruciating pain. At that moment, David remembered what he had already achieved. That memory let him shift attention away from the negative inner voice and move past physical suffering. This kind of mental power is not only for competitions and training.

When you face obstacles at work or in school, use this tactic to take control of your doubts. Mentally list your achievements. Picture how you overcame a specific obstacle. Imagine the challenges you can meet. It will help you be ready.

chapters.title: Starting from a difficult place
chapters.text:
David Goggins' life was never easy, even as a child. Although his family looked perfectly normal on the outside, they had hidden troubles. Goggins' father, Trunnis, was an extremely violent man. He owned a roller-skating rink in Buffalo and ran a bar above it, where he forced his wife Jackie and their two kids to work. Carrying out orders until late at night, six-year-old David often fell asleep during class.

Exhausting work and lack of sleep were not the only problems. Trunnis brutally punished his sons and wife for the smallest mistake or hint of disobedience. Physical and psychological violence, fear, and pain were part of daily life. Although Jackie tried to keep her children's lives stable, their father always found a way to disrupt them.

No matter where you started, you have the power to change everything.

David remembers the moment when, after another violent beating, his mother called the police. Instead of the rescue they hoped for, they watched their father walk the officers back to their car, laughing and complaining about his marriage. There seemed to be no way out of the nightmare.

Then one day, Jackie found the strength to end it. After telling her husband she was leaving, she offered her sons the choice to come with her. They packed a few clothes quickly and drove to the small town in southern Indiana where Jackie's parents lived.

They had no money, because all the profits from the rink and the bar went to their father's account. Once, they even had to break a piggy bank to buy food and put gas in the car. Their life was not cloudless, but the most important thing was escaping the devil they lived with.

David went to a new school, where he gradually caught up to his classmates. It looked like a brighter stretch had arrived, but this was only the start of more challenges to come.

chapters.title: Confronting a hard reality and choosing to fight
chapters.text:
Third grade at a new school was a complete shock for David. Studying was extremely difficult, and his new teacher, who yelled at him for his poor performance, only added to his stress. Out of fear of being sent to a school for special students, David started cheating. He copied homework and tests that helped lift his grades.

As a teenager, David ran into another harsh reality: racism. During a walk with his cousin, the passengers of a pickup truck shouted a racial slur at them.

It was the first, but not the last, time David heard that word. He even received a note with threats from classmates who promised to kill him. Childhood trauma and a hostile environment fed his despair and pain. Yet, despite all the sadness, he still had one hope. He dreamed of enlisting in the Air Force.

As a member of the Civil Air Patrol, the civilian auxiliary, he heard the story of a pararescueman who survived horrific injuries and nearly died but returned to duty. It convinced David that human potential is limitless. Still, he had a long way to go to reach his dream. After failing the ASVAB, a standardized test for the military, David knew he had to change something.

Tomorrow depends on the decisions you make today.

He studied his reflection in the mirror and was honest with himself about who he was, a boy who read at a third-grade level and cheated on his work. But he knew what he wanted and was ready to put in the effort. From that moment, the ritual with the mirror became a tradition. He started by making his bed and taking care of his appearance. Then he focused on studying and training to chase his dream.

These steps might help you if you are going through a transformation. Write down all your dreams and stick them on your mirror. Identify your current stage in reaching your goal and decide on the next step. Make a separate note for every small step and peel it off the mirror after you finish it.

chapters.title: It is never too late to rewrite your story
chapters.text:
The Air Force was meant to be a fresh start for David, and he did everything he could to make the most of that chance. He was strong at push-ups, running, and squatting, but one course was too tough: water confidence. During the ten-week program, he went through special evolutions to learn to stay calm in water under stress. Every day brought another humiliation in the pool, but David kept fighting.

His dream stumbled when, during a medical exam, doctors discovered he was a carrier of the Sickle Cell Trait. They thought it could raise his risk of exercise-related sudden death. David felt relieved when he was released from training, but the doctors reversed their decision and gave him the choice to continue. He had already missed half of the trials, and returning meant starting from the beginning. When the Master Sergeant asked about his final decision, David could not meet his eyes. He had given up, using Sickle Cell Trait as an excuse to leave.

Get out of your comfort zone regularly to train your brain for new victories.

After leaving the program, David put his military career on hold and went to work at Ecolab, disinfecting food establishments. He buried his Air Force dream and hidden sadness in the gym and in food. His life was falling apart, but it was not the end. The day he took his destiny back was no different from any other. He finished his night shift and drove home, having grabbed a milkshake out of habit. Turning on the TV, David caught snippets of a broadcast about the Navy SEALs, the toughest force in the world. It was a turning point, and he knew what to do. He threw the milkshake in the trash, put on his sneakers, and went for a run. It was the first step to a new beginning.

For some context, David Goggins worked with Chris Kyle during SEAL training, the most respected sniper in Navy history, who later wrote American Sniper, a book that became a hit movie starring Bradley Cooper.

chapters.title: From struggle to inner peace
chapters.text:
The military was not the only place where David Goggins pushed himself. To raise money for the families of fallen comrades, he took part in one of the toughest ultra-marathons in the world, Badwater. But first, he had to finish easier races to earn a spot. Without enough training, he ran a hundred miles in twenty-four hours, nearly dying of kidney failure. So how did he do it? David uses the 40 percent rule, which says you still have 60 percent left in reserve when you think you have hit your limit. By dropping doubts, letting go of self-limiting stories, and raising your pain tolerance, you can tap into your full potential at a critical moment.

Across his life, David always sought to push beyond his abilities. This drive made him a famous ultra-marathoner and let him break the Guinness record for pull-ups. He did not give up, even when he had heart surgery for a congenital condition. The fact that he could achieve so much with a heart working at only half capacity is incredible.

Yet one day, David Goggins' body pushed back. He could not get out of bed, and every muscle ached. Doctors could not find the cause, and David felt he was slowly fading. Lying exhausted on his bed, he thought about his past and all he had achieved, and finally realized how demanding he had been of himself. David recognized that everything he did was for acceptance. His rage and fear of worthlessness had driven him, and now he could see it clearly.

David had finally found the clue to his illness. After years of exhausting work, his body needed care. Remembering a stretching session from his SEAL training, he began to exercise. It was painful, but it worked and restored strength to his body.

Even if everyone says your dream is impossible, ask, what if? Then start your journey.

After finishing his military career, David Goggins did not stop. At age forty-three, he began a professional career as a wildland firefighter. He continues to believe in the limitlessness of human potential and is ready to do what everyone else thinks is impossible.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
There is an idea in Buddhism that life is suffering. We all have to walk through hardship and pain. Sometimes, we try to soften life's blows and stay in our comfort zone, but it can turn into a prison. You have to step past the familiar and challenge yourself to achieve your dreams. Yes, it is a tough path that calls for daily hard work and struggle.

People may say your goals are unrealistic, but only you know your own reality. The most important conversations happen inside, and you are the person who can change everything. When in doubt, remember David Goggins, the man who expanded his potential. You can do the same.

Try this. Reflect on your past victories to boost confidence in present hurdles. Push yourself a little more each day, even if it is just one extra step or minute. Practice self-awareness, since understanding your why can be a powerful motivator. In moments of despair, thank yourself for the lessons learned on the paths you have already taken.

Made it to the end?
